note_data.title: Data types and structures
keyPoints: **Data type** is a classification that specifies the type of value a variable can hold and the operations that can be performed on it
**Array** is a data structure that stores multiple values of the same data type in contiguous memory locations using a single identifier
**Record** is a composite data type that groups together related data items of different data types under a single identifier
**Linked list** uses nodes containing data and pointers, where each node contains data and a pointer to the next node in the sequence
**Stack** follows Last In First Out (LIFO) principle with operations push (add to top) and pop (remove from top)
**Queue** follows First In First Out (FIFO) principle with operations enqueue (add to rear) and dequeue (remove from front)
Arrays provide **direct access** to elements using index positions, making data retrieval faster
Linked lists provide **dynamic memory allocation** allowing the size to change during program execution
**Static data structures** have fixed size determined at compile time, while **dynamic data structures** can change size during runtime
Each data structure has specific **time complexity** characteristics for insertion, deletion and searching operations

definitions.definition: A data structure that stores multiple elements of the same data type in contiguous memory locations, accessed using index positions
A composite data type that groups together related data items of different data types, accessed using field names
A linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes, each containing data and a pointer to the next node
A linear data structure that follows Last In First Out (LIFO) principle where elements are added and removed from the same end
A linear data structure that follows First In First Out (FIFO) principle where elements are added at the rear and removed from the front

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give similarities AND differences between two data structures
Give characteristics and how operations work step by step

modelAnswer.question: A Pakistani university uses a queue data structure to manage student registration. Explain how the enqueue and dequeue operations work. [6]
modelAnswer.answer: Enqueue operation adds a new student to the rear of the queue. The rear pointer is incremented to point to the next available position. The new student data is inserted at this rear position. Dequeue operation removes a student from the front of the queue. The student data at the front position is retrieved and returned. The front pointer is incremented to point to the next student in the queue.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Enqueue adds to rear of queue
Mark 3: Data inserted at rear position
Mark 5: Data retrieved from front position

commonMistakes: Never say 'queue adds at the back' — always say 'enqueue adds to the rear'
Students often confuse stack push/pop with queue enqueue/dequeue operations
Never say 'linked list is faster' — specify what operation (insertion/deletion/searching)
Students forget to mention pointers when describing linked lists
Never say 'array size cannot change' — say 'static arrays have fixed size determined at declaration'
examinerTips: Data structures appear in Paper 2 every year, worth 8-12 marks typically
Always draw labelled diagrams for linked lists showing nodes and pointers
Learn the exact operations for each structure - push/pop for stack, enqueue/dequeue for queue
Compare time complexities when asked about efficiency of data structures
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Stack diagram showing LIFO with push/pop operations at top. Queue diagram showing FIFO with enqueue at rear, dequeue at front. Linked list showing nodes with data fields and pointer fields connecting to next nodes.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain the difference between static and dynamic data structures and give examples of each with justification for use
O/N 2022 P1 Q12 (2 marks) — tested knowledge of appropriate data types for storing different values like age, name, and price with reasoning
M/J 2022 P2 Q5 (8 marks) — required students to design a record structure for a library system and explain validation checks for each field
O/N 2023 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — asked candidates to trace through array operations and identify errors in pseudocode for searching algorithms

workedExample.question: A school needs to store student data. Design an appropriate data structure and justify the data types chosen for: StudentID, Name, DateOfBirth, TestScores (5 subjects). (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the record structure needed - create a RECORD called Student
Step 2: Choose StudentID as INTEGER (unique identifier, used for calculations/comparisons)
Step 3: Choose Name as STRING (contains letters and possibly spaces/hyphens)
Step 4: Choose DateOfBirth as DATE (specialized data type for date operations and validation)
Step 5: Choose TestScores as ARRAY[1:5] OF REAL (multiple values of same type, allows decimals for precise marking)
workedExample.answer: RECORD Student: StudentID (INTEGER) - unique numerical identifier for database operations; Name (STRING) - text data requiring variable length; DateOfBirth (DATE) - enables age calculations and date validation; TestScores as ARRAY[1:5] OF REAL - stores multiple decimal values efficiently with index access.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct record structure, 1 mark each for appropriate data type choice (4 types), 1 mark for clear justification of choices

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused static arrays with dynamic structures' — always specify whether array size is fixed at compile-time or can change during execution

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often chose inappropriate data types without justification' — always explain WHY you chose INTEGER over REAL, or STRING over CHAR, considering storage and operations needed

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates failed to show understanding of 2D arrays vs arrays of records' — clearly distinguish between ARRAY[1:10,1:5] and ARRAY[1:10] OF RECORD when designing data structures
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a memory representation diagram showing: 1) Array with indexed elements (showing index numbers 0-4 and corresponding values), 2) Record structure with labeled fields and their data types, 3) 2D array as rows and columns with coordinate references [row,column]. Include arrows showing pointer relationships and memory addresses where relevant.
note_data.quickRecap: Data types define what values can be stored (INTEGER, REAL, STRING, BOOLEAN, DATE) and operations allowed. Arrays store multiple values of same type with index access. Records group related data of different types using field names. Choose data types based on required operations, storage efficiency, and validation needs.
